findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is bilateral enlargement of the extraocular muscle bellies. The enlargement classically and preferentially affects the inferior, medial, superior, and lateral rectus muscles in that order.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include an increase in the volume of the intraorbital fat and enlargement of the lacrimal glands, which can contribute to significant proptosis. There may also be associated stretching or compression of the optic nerve at the orbital apex.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of thickening involving the myotendinous junctions, which classically remain spared. Furthermore, there is no discrete focal orbital mass, bone destruction, or extension into the cavernous sinus.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the bilateral, symmetric enlargement of multiple extraocular muscle bellies with characteristic sparing of their tendinous insertions.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Thyroid-associated orbitopathy, given the bilateral extraocular muscle enlargement that strictly spares the myotendinous junctions. This autoimmune inflammatory condition is the most common cause of proptosis in adults, classically affecting the inferior and medial rectus muscles first.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Orbital pseudotumor, however this typically presents as a unilateral, painful condition that characteristically involves both the muscle belly and the tendinous insertion. Furthermore, pseudotumor often involves other orbital structures with ill-defined inflammatory changes rather than isolated muscle belly enlargement.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lymphoma, given that it can present as an indolent, painless mass or diffuse extraocular muscle infiltration in an older adult, and missing it would drastically delay systemic therapy. However, orbital lymphoma typically forms a more discrete, moldable mass that adapts to orbital structures and does not classically follow the specific muscle distribution seen here.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the orbital apex for any signs of optic nerve compression or stretching that could threaten vision and require urgent surgical decompression.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to monitor the degree of proptosis and muscle thickening in response to corticosteroid therapy.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the orbits if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the optic nerve for compressive optic neuropathy and assessing disease activity using fluid-sensitive sequences.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution coronal and axial fat-suppressed T2-weighted or STIR sequences to identify active inflammation and edema within the muscles, alongside pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to assess the degree of enhancement and optic nerve integrity.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Ophthalmology and Endocrinology MDT. My report would clearly document the specific extraocular muscles involved, the severity of proptosis, and critically, the presence or absence of apical optic nerve compression to guide urgent medical or surgical management.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is bilateral enlargement of the extraocular muscle bellies. The enlargement classically and preferentially affects the inferior, medial, superior, and lateral rectus muscles in that order. Supporting features include an increase in the volume of the intraorbital fat and enlargement of the lacrimal glands, which can contribute to significant proptosis. There may also be associated stretching or compression of the optic nerve at the orbital apex. Importantly, there is no evidence of thickening involving the myotendinous junctions, which classically remain spared. Furthermore, there is no discrete focal orbital mass, bone destruction, or extension into the cavernous sinus. The feature that most influences my thinking is the bilateral, symmetric enlargement of multiple extraocular muscle bellies with characteristic sparing of their tendinous insertions.

The appearances are most in keeping with Thyroid-associated orbitopathy, given the bilateral extraocular muscle enlargement that strictly spares the myotendinous junctions. This autoimmune inflammatory condition is the most common cause of proptosis in adults, classically affecting the inferior and medial rectus muscles first. The principle differential would be Orbital pseudotumor, however this typically presents as a unilateral, painful condition that characteristically involves both the muscle belly and the tendinous insertion. Furthermore, pseudotumor often involves other orbital structures with ill-defined inflammatory changes rather than isolated muscle belly enlargement. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lymphoma, given that it can present as an indolent, painless mass or diffuse extraocular muscle infiltration in an older adult, and missing it would drastically delay systemic therapy. However, orbital lymphoma typically forms a more discrete, moldable mass that adapts to orbital structures and does not classically follow the specific muscle distribution seen here.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the orbital apex for any signs of optic nerve compression or stretching that could threaten vision and require urgent surgical decompression. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to monitor the degree of proptosis and muscle thickening in response to corticosteroid therapy. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the orbits if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the optic nerve for compressive optic neuropathy and assessing disease activity using fluid-sensitive sequences. I would specify high-resolution coronal and axial fat-suppressed T2-weighted or STIR sequences to identify active inflammation and edema within the muscles, alongside pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to assess the degree of enhancement and optic nerve integrity. This should go to the Ophthalmology and Endocrinology MDT. My report would clearly document the specific extraocular muscles involved, the severity of proptosis, and critically, the presence or absence of apical optic nerve compression to guide urgent medical or surgical management.
